SUMMARY:
5 Top Synthetic Biology Startups | StartUs Insights Image 2: Explore 5 Top Synthetic Biology Startups _We analyzed 486 synthetic biology startups impacting the biotech sector.​ Cysbio, Siolta Therapeutics, Kinnva, Genecis & Asimov develop 5 top solutions including cell factories, bioplastics, and aquaculture feed solutions. Global Startup Heat Map highlights 5 Top Synthetic Biology Companies Through the Big Data & Artificial Intelligence (AI)-powered StartUs Insights Discovery Platform, covering over 3 790 000+ startups & scaleups globally, we identified 400+ synthetic biology startups. Image 3: Synthetic-Biology-Startups-Biotechnology-Heat-Map-StartUs-Insights-noresize The synthetic biology companies showcased in this report are only a small sample of all startups we identified through our data-driven startup scouting approach. Curious about startups advancing protein engineering through AI, synthetic biology, and custom design platforms?

### Cysbio constructs Bacterial Cell Factories

The biotech industry has long used genetically modified bacteria to produce highly valuable biochemicals for pharma, materials, and food, among other industries. However, it remains an uphill task to make microbes produce chemicals that they cannot naturally synthesize. The startup’s proprietary processes produce biochemicals such as amino acids for pharma, food, and feed markets. With metabolic and enzyme engineering, The startup imparts novel functionalities to biochemicals with its patented sulfation technology that produces sulfated biochemicals. This solution finds applications in antifouling agents, nutraceutical ingredients, and precursors for functional polymers.

Imagine a world in which all the planes in the sky are fueled by algae and the very breath you exhale can be turned into food. Everything, from plastic bags to clothing, is [completely biodegradable](https://builtin.com/greentech/environmental-technology-examples). This may sound like science fiction right now, but it’s a future[synthetic biology companies](https://builtin.com/biotech/biotech-companies) across the country are working to bring to fruition — and it’s not as distant as you might think.

What Is Synthetic Biology?
--------------------------

Synthetic biology is the practice of genetically modifying organisms by adding chunks of DNA into an organism’s genome. Organisms then gain new abilities that make them useful for various purposes, like microbes that enhance food products or enzymes that detect diseases.

Just like rearranging ones and zeros on a screen can alter a digital product, changing the chemical code contained in DNA can alter a biological system. And these newly formed microorganisms can, in theory, make many of the same things that industrial processes handle now. With synthetic biology, companies can make an infinite amount of raw materials from scratch, cell by cell, allowing us to manufacture the things we depend on in a much more sustainable way. Indeed, just as data and cloud computing companies brought us into the digital era, synthetic biology is forging an[entirely new](https://builtin.com/data-science/microbiome-research-data-science) production and sustainability frontier.

With a reported market value of more than [$17 billion](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2023/02/23/2614571/0/en/Synthetic-Biology-Global-Market-Report-2023.html), the industry seems to have hit its stride. While there is still a long way to go to make many of these products and processes commercially available, the progress synthetic biology has made so far is spawning an entire ecosystem of companies that are working to revolutionize the material world as we know it. And these[companies](https://builtin.com/raleigh/biotech-companies-in-rtp) are doing it in ways that are more[environmentally friendly](https://builtin.com/greentech) because they use fewer resources and fossil fuels.

Companies are actively engaged in bioengineering crops with enhanced nutritional content and prolonged shelf life to meet these growing challenges.

These designer organisms can produce biofuels, enzymes, and other valuable compounds, offering solutions to environmental challenges and industrial needs. Companies are harnessing this potential to drive innovation in various sectors.

**Speeding up of Commercialization Pathways**: Synthetic biology streamlines the development and commercialization of bioproducts through rapid prototyping, automation, and standardized parts. Agile approaches adopted by companies facilitate faster product iterations, allowing innovations to reach the market swiftly.

**Growth in Synthetic Biology Foundries**: Synthetic biology foundries serve as collaborative hubs for research and development, providing access to shared resources, expertise, and standardized biological parts. Companies leverage these facilities to accelerate their projects, benefiting from collective knowledge and infrastructure.

**Increasing Research and Development Investment by Pharma Companies**: Pharmaceutical companies are increasingly recognizing the potential of synthetic biology and investing in research and development efforts. Collaborations between industry giants and synthetic biology startups drive innovation in the development of novel therapeutics, vaccines, and diagnostics, promising advancements in healthcare.

**Top Ten Suppliers of Synthetic Biology Technology Segmentation****Analysis**

**By Product Type Segmentation**

Enabling Products encompass foundational tools like DNA synthesizers, gene editing kits, and cloning vectors, essential for manipulating genetic material in synthetic biology research. Biological Components refer to standardized genetic parts such as promoters and enhancers, enabling the construction of custom genetic circuits. Integrated Systems combine biological components into functional units, like gene expression platforms and metabolic pathways, to create robust biological functions. Enabled Products represent end-user applications leveraging synthetic biology innovations, such as biofuels and biopharmaceuticals.

**By Application Segmentation**

R&D Tools aid in gene synthesis, genome editing, and pathway engineering for scientific investigations. Diagnostics/Pharmaceuticals utilize synthetic biology for diagnostic tests, drug development, and personalized medicine. Agriculture employs synthetic biology to enhance crop traits, pest resistance, and yield. Chemicals involve the production of specialty chemicals, enzymes, and bio-based materials. Energy applications focus on biofuels, microbial fuel cells, and algae-based energy systems. Food & Beverages utilize synthetic biology for food production, flavor enhancement, and nutritional content improvement.

**By Region Segmentation**

North America serves as a hub for research, development, and commercialization of synthetic biology, particularly in the United States and Canada. Latin America shows emerging interest, especially in Brazil, Mexico, and Argentina. Europe boasts a strong ecosystem with leading countries like the United Kingdom, Germany, and the Netherlands. APAC witnesses rapid growth, primarily driven by China, India, and Japan, with significant investments and government support.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Synthetic biology startups vary in sector focus; some target biochemicals for industrial applications, while others develop therapeutics for healthcare or innovations in aquaculture for sustainable food production.

Startups employ diverse technological approaches, from metabolic engineering in biochemicals to advanced CRISPR techniques for gene editing in therapeutics, enhancing product efficacy and sustainability.

Market strategies differ by sector; biochemicals often emphasize industrial partnerships, therapeutics focus on clinical trials and regulatory compliance, while aquaculture startups highlight sustainability and feed efficiency.

Regulatory hurdles are prevalent across sectors, with therapeutics facing stringent FDA approval processes, whereas biochemicals and aquaculture navigate environmental regulations and safety assessments.

Each startup targets specific customer bases; biochemicals cater to industries like pharmaceuticals and materials, while therapeutics focus on healthcare providers, and aquaculture targets fish farmers and distributors.
